channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In Huge Blow to Democrats, Virginia Court Strikes Down House Map
item.description: The decision is a major victory for Republicans, wiping away a measure approved by voters to allow Democrats to gain as many as four House seats in the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson, Nick Corasaniti and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Steve Earley/The Virginian-Pilot, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Virginia Supreme Court in Richmond, Va.

item.title: Democratic Gubernatorial Candidate Rob Sand Is Worrying Iowa Republicans
item.description: Rob Sand, Iowa’s auditor, is campaigning for governor with a pragmatic message that eschews strict partisanship.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Clayton Steward for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rob Sand, Iowa’s auditor, has amassed a formidable war chest, out-raising all his opponents combined.

item.title: California Republican Incumbents Race to the Right as Redistricting Forces Standoff
item.description: Forced to fight for political survival because of redistricting, Ken Calvert and Young Kim, both Republicans, are accusing each other of being insufficiently MAGA.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning

item.title: Trump Calls Iran Strikes a ‘Trifle,’ and the Latest on the Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Simon Romero and Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Even after the United States and Iran exchanged fire in the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, President Trump insisted that the cease-fire was still in effect and dismissed the Iranian attacks as a “trifle.”

item.title: Federal Reserve Turns Focus to Inflation as Job Market Stabilizes
item.description: April’s report showed employers added more jobs than expected, supporting the central bank’s view that it can afford to hold interest rates steady.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie/The New York Times
item.media:description: Higher prices could eventually temper consumer spending, causing a slowdown that could result in job losses.

item.title: ‘Fired From Retirement’: Financially Insecure, Older Americans Return to the Grind
item.description: People return to work for many reasons, but the biggest is financial need. As costs remain high, more retirees could be looking to re-enter the job market.
item.dc:creator: Kurtis Lee
item.category: Center for Retirement Research at Boston College
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pat Archer retired several years ago but found that her Social Security and small pension couldn’t keep up with her bills. She found part-time work — in a retirement home.

item.title: Emirates Doubles Down on U.S. and Israeli Ties Amid Iran War
item.description: The United Arab Emirates has borne the brunt of Iranian attacks throughout the war, hardening the government’s resolve to reassess its list of friends and foes.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Emirates leader, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed, and President Trump in Abu Dhabi, last year.

item.title: U.A.E. Expels Pakistani Workers, as Pakistan’s Peacemaking Creates a Rift
item.description: As Pakistan mediates between the U.S. and Iran, its ties to the Emirates have deteriorated. Pakistani workers say they are now being sent home en masse.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier, Zia ur-Rehman and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Asim Hafeez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Travelers at Pakistan’s Islamabad International Airport this month.

item.title: Pentagon Releases Files on U.F.O.s
item.description: The initial files are murky images that show what could be anything. The government said more would be released on a rolling basis.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Trade Court Rules Trump’s 10% Global Tariff Is Illegal
item.description: A panel of federal judges found that President Trump could not legally impose the tariff on most imports.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: A court found that President Trump had wrongly invoked a decades-old trade law when he applied the duties in February.

item.title: Taiwan Breaks Political Deadlock to Approve $25 Billion U.S. Arms Purchase
item.description: With time running out, Taiwanese lawmakers approved funds for buying U.S. weapons, fearing they could lose support if they didn’t act now.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:credit: I-Hwa Cheng/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A Patriot missile system deployed at a park in Taipei during a military exercise last year. The Patriot is one of the most sought-after air-defense systems on the weapons market.

item.title: At the Venice Biennale, an American Artist Plops His Sculptures on a World Stage
item.description: They may be bronze and marble, but Alma Allen’s artworks at the Venice Biennale are as intangible as air.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alma Allen on an international stage in Venice. Right, “Not Yet Titled” (2014), yule marble. Left, “Not Yet Titled” (2020), bronze, at the U.S. Pavilion.

item.title: A Very American Controversy at the Venicle Biennale
item.description: Trump has taken an active role in the arts in his second term, which may be evident in the work on display at the Venice Biennale — depending on how you look at it.
item.dc:creator: M.H. Miller
item.media:credit: Andrea Frazetta for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeffrey Uslip with a sculpture by Alma Allen at the U.S. Pavilion.

item.title: What’s at Stake in the UK’s Local Elections
item.description: The voting in England, Scotland and Wales is the biggest electoral test for Keir Starmer since he became prime minister in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Leon Neal/Getty Images
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain spoke on Friday after local election results began rolling in.

item.title: Purported Epstein Suicide Note Has Echoes of His Other Messages
item.description: The New York Times has not authenticated the note. But some of its phrases are similar or identical to ones Jeffrey Epstein used in emails and another handwritten letter.
item.dc:creator: Steve Eder and Eve Edelheit
item.media:credit: New York State Sex Offender Registry, via Associated Press

item.title: U.S. Schools Face a Crisis as the Number of Children Drops
item.description: With fewer students, many public school districts are confronting unfilled classrooms, and hard choices about school closures.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Mervosh, Francesca Paris and Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Many Russians in No Mood for Celebration on Kremlin’s Biggest Day of the Year
item.description: As the country heads into Victory Day, the economy is stalling, internet restrictions are growing and the Kremlin is fearing Ukrainian attacks.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Nanna Heitmann
item.category: Communist Party of the Russian Federation
item.media:description: Red Square in Moscow. The Victory Day procession, one of the year’s biggest events, is staged in the heart of Kremlin power.

item.title: A.I. Populism Is Here. And No One Is Ready.
item.description: Silicon Valley oligarchs worried about the risks their technology posed to the world. They forgot about people.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells

item.title: Health Officials Race to Track Hantavirus Cruise Ship Outbreak but Predict ‘Limited’ Spread
item.description: A Dutch flight attendant and French national were among the latest to be tested in connection with a deadly outbreak on a cruise ship. But an analysis in South Africa showed no mutations in viral samples.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses, Aimee Ortiz and Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: Danilson Sequeira/Reuters
item.media:description: An ambulance during an evacuation of suspected hantavirus patients, in Praia, Cape Verde, on Wednesday.

item.title: What to Know About the Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: Health authorities said three passengers from the MV Hondius had died after showing symptoms of the rare disease.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Jin Yu Young
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.

item.title: Gates Around Washington Square Park? Greenwich Village Is Divided.
item.description: The park in Manhattan, known for its regal arch and freewheeling spirit, has a hard-to-enforce midnight curfew.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Yuki Iwamura/Associated Press
item.media:description: Washington Square Park in Manhattan is supposed to be off limits from midnight to 6 a.m., but people often ignore this curfew.

item.title: New York’s Budget Deal Is Still Hazy. Here Are 5 Key Questions.
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul claimed victory for a state budget agreement that is already five weeks overdue, and clearly still needs more work.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Kathy Hochul, who is facing re-election this year, announced the framework of a $268 billion state budget on Thursday, but acknowledged that some issues still needed to be resolved.

item.title: The Many Love Lives of Ted Turner
item.description: Known as a playboy, the media mogul gave his paramours and three ex-wives plenty of stories to tell. He also managed to stay friendly with many of them.
item.dc:creator: Sadiba Hasan
item.media:credit: Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Jane Fonda, the Academy Award-winning actress, wrote in her memoir that Turner cheated on her a month after they married.

item.title: Mark Zuckerberg Is Running Meta Into the Ground
item.description: Meta has commenced a long, slow slide into irrelevance.
item.dc:creator: Julia Angwin

item.title: America’s Arrested Development
item.description: The American economy and the climate would both benefit from permitting reform.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Illustration by Rebecca Chew/The New York Times

item.title: Going for Broke
item.description: Why does the public seem unbothered by the national debt?
item.dc:creator: Evan Gorelick
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: In Washington, D.C.

item.title: Late Night Recaps Rubio’s Peacemaking Visit to Pope Leo
item.description: Jimmy Fallon joked that President Trump and Pope Leo are putting Marco Rubio, who is Catholic, “in the middle and seeing which parent he goes to.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Four Men Found Guilty of Haiti President’s Assassination
item.description: President Jovenel Moïse, 53, was gunned down in his bedroom in July 2021. His killing unleashed a yearslong spiral of gang violence and mayhem in Haiti.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural of Jovenel Moïse near his home in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, where he was assassinated in 2021.

item.title: Philip Caputo, Who Wrote Blistering Vietnam War Memoir, Dies at 84
item.description: “A Rumor of War,” about his service as a Marine Corps infantry officer and published in 1977, relentlessly detailed “the things men do in war and the things war does to them.”
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.media:credit: Frank Lennon/Toronto Star, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Philip Caputo in 1987. “To call it the best book about Vietnam is to trivialize it,” the novelist and screenwriter John Gregory Dunne wrote in his review of “A Rumor of War.” “Heartbreaking, terrifying and enraging, it belongs to the literature of men at arms.”

item.title: The French Couple That Tried to Burgle 29 Churches in 3 Months
item.description: A spree of thefts last summer heightened alarm in France over protection of cultural and rural heritage.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter and Ana Castelain
item.media:credit: Andrea Mantovani for The New York Times
item.media:description: The tabernacle in the church of Chamouille, France, which was robbed last summer. Some priests oversee as many as 50 churches.

item.title: South Africa’s President Faces Potential Impeachment After Court Decision
item.description: Judges ordered Parliament to convene a committee to consider removing President Cyril Ramaphosa over a case involving money stolen from a couch at his farm six years ago.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon
item.media:credit: Siphiwe Sibeko/Reuters
item.media:description: At the Constitutional Court in Johannesburg on Friday.
